primary_diagnosis.name: Innocent (functional) heart murmur - Still's murmur - in an asymptomatic, thriving child; structurally normal heart

pathophysiology_summary: An innocent (functional) murmur - most commonly a Still's murmur in this age group - is the audible sound of normal blood flow through a structurally normal heart, heard when vigorous flow in a thin-chested, active child produces low-frequency vibration, classically a soft, short, musical/vibratory systolic ejection murmur (grade <=2/6) best heard at the left lower sternal border. Its benign nature is signalled by acoustic and clinical features: it is soft and early-systolic (not loud, harsh or holosystolic), it decreases in intensity on standing (as venous return falls), the second heart sound splits normally (not fixed and widely split as in an atrial septal defect), there are no added sounds/clicks/gallops, the apex is non-displaced, and the peripheral pulses are normal and synchronous. Crucially, the child is completely asymptomatic with normal exercise tolerance, no cyanosis/clubbing, normal growth and development, and no family history of sudden cardiac death or congenital heart disease - the reassuring 'no red flags' picture. Pathological murmurs would instead be suggested by symptoms (exertional syncope, chest pain or breathlessness, poor feeding/failure to thrive, cyanosis), a loud/harsh or diastolic murmur, a thrill, an abnormal S2, radiation to the carotids, or abnormal pulses. Innocent murmurs are extremely common in healthy children and typically become inaudible with growth. Because auscultation cannot be performed over telehealth, the diagnosis is confirmed by routine in-person clinical examination (echocardiography only if clinically indicated), and the correct action is appropriate reassurance with routine confirmation - not an emergency presentation - while retaining safety-netting for genuine red flags.

differential.evidence_basis: An incidental murmur in a completely asymptomatic, thriving, active child is most likely innocent.
Considered for a childhood murmur, but a fixed widely-split S2 (its hallmark) is absent and the murmur decreases on standing.
A VSD murmur is loud/harsh and holosystolic with a possible thrill, unlike this soft early-systolic murmur.
Would radiate to the carotids with altered pulses and exertional symptoms - none present here - but is the pathology not to miss in an asymptomatic murmur.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should recognise the benign picture while screening for pathological features, and must not over-triage a well child to emergency assessment.

differential.diagnosis: Innocent (Still's) murmur in a structurally normal heart
differential.evidence_basis: Complete absence of symptoms (DI-001), no cyanosis/feeding issues (DI-002), normal growth (DI-003) and no concerning family history (DI-004) strongly support a benign innocent murmur.
Normal physiological S2 splitting and a murmur that decreases on standing argue against ASD.
The soft early-systolic character without a thrill is not a VSD.
No carotid radiation, normal pulses and full asymptomatic exertion argue against significant aortic stenosis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is a benign innocent murmur: reassure, no restriction, routine in-person confirmation, and safety-net genuine red flags - do not over-investigate or over-triage.

differential.diagnosis: Innocent (Still's) murmur - benign, pending routine in-person auscultation for confirmation
differential.evidence_basis: Working conclusion for telehealth. Auscultation cannot be done remotely; routine in-person examination confirms the benign features (echocardiography only if clinically indicated).

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is a likely innocent murmur needing routine GP confirmation and reassurance, with red-flag safety-netting - explicitly not an emergency.

red_flags.description: Pathological-murmur red flags (exertional syncope, chest pain or breathlessness, cyanosis, poor feeding/failure to thrive) - all ABSENT
red_flags.clinical_significance: The absence of these features strongly supports a benign innocent murmur; were any present, urgent paediatric cardiology assessment would be needed. They must still be safety-netted.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If a red flag later develops and is not acted on, an underlying structural lesion could be missed.

red_flags.description: Benign auscultatory features - soft short systolic ejection murmur (<=2/6) at the left lower sternal border, decreasing on standing, with normal S2 splitting and no added sounds; normal apex and pulses
red_flags.clinical_significance: These clinician-elicited features are characteristic of an innocent murmur and distinguish it from pathological murmurs; auscultation cannot be performed over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires in-person auscultation; the correct action is routine in-person confirmation.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-triaging a well child to the emergency department
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Severe parental anxiety can pressure an unnecessary overnight paediatric ED visit; a well, thriving child with an incidental murmur does not need emergency assessment.
Ordering an echocardiogram for every innocent murmur is unnecessary; it is reserved for cases with concerning features or for parental reassurance in discussion with the GP.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Dismissiveness that fails to calm anxiety
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Curt reassurance without addressing the parents' specific fear (sudden death in sleep) can leave them frightened and prompt an inappropriate ED visit.
Restricting sport in a well child with an innocent murmur is harmful and unwarranted; normal activity should be encouraged.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Cardiac auscultation to characterise the murmur (intensity, timing, response to standing, S2 splitting)
Palpation for a thrill and assessment of the apex beat
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Reassure that an incidental murmur in an asymptomatic, thriving child is very likely innocent; arrange routine in-person GP examination within 1-2 weeks to confirm the benign features (echocardiography only if clinically indicated), encourage normal activity/sport with no restriction, and safety-net genuine red flags - explicitly NOT an emergency department presentation tonight.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Over-triaging a well child to the emergency department, over-investigating, or conversely dismissing the parents so poorly that they present inappropriately, would all be errors; and any genuine red flag (syncope, exertional symptoms, cyanosis) must not be ignored.
